screens.prompt: Your recorded burst is a real, physically measurable voltage — nothing imaginary about it. Yet every serious signal-processing treatment of it will lean on complex numbers, $e^{j\theta} = \cos\theta + j\sin\theta$. Does bringing in complex numbers mean you're no longer describing the real, physical signal?
choices: Yes — once complex numbers appear, you've left the physical signal behind
No — complex exponentials are a bookkeeping tool that makes real, physical oscillations far easier to manipulate algebraically
screens.reveal: No — the physical signal stays exactly as real as it always was. What changes is how conveniently you can *describe* it. $\cos(\omega t) = \tfrac12\big(e^{j\omega t} + e^{-j\omega t}\big)$ — a real cosine is literally the sum of two complex exponentials spinning in opposite directions, and their imaginary parts cancel exactly. Complex exponentials let you replace trigonometric identity-juggling (product-to-sum formulas, phase-shift headaches) with plain exponent algebra: multiplying exponentials just adds their exponents. Nothing about the underlying physical voltage becomes imaginary — you've just chosen a more convenient set of building blocks to describe it with.

screens.prompt: Using Euler's formula $e^{j\theta} = \cos\theta + j\sin\theta$, what is the real part of $e^{j\pi}$?
screens.successFeedback: Right — e^{jπ} = cos(π) + j·sin(π) = −1 + j·0 = −1. This is Euler's identity in its famous form: e^{jπ} + 1 = 0.
hints: cos(π) and sin(π) are two values you likely already know.

screens.prompt: Geometrically, what does e^{jωt} represent as t increases?

choices.text: A unit-length vector (phasor) in the complex plane, rotating counterclockwise at angular rate ω
A vector that grows longer over time
choices.feedback: e^{jωt} always has magnitude exactly 1 for real ω and t — it rotates, but its length never changes. Growth/decay would require a real exponential factor like e^{σt} multiplied in separately.

choices.text: A fixed point that doesn't move
choices.feedback: It moves continuously — that's the whole point of the exponential having an imaginary argument that grows with t. A fixed, non-moving point would just be a constant, not e^{jωt}.

screens.successFeedback: Exactly — this rotating-phasor picture is the geometric heart of every Fourier-based technique in this course: a signal built from complex exponentials is built from combinations of these constant-speed rotating arrows.

screens.body: $\cos(\omega t)$ is built from *two* phasors spinning in opposite directions (at $+\omega$ and $-\omega$) — redundant information, since for a real signal the negative-frequency half is always just the mirror image (complex conjugate) of the positive-frequency half. The **analytic signal** keeps only the positive-frequency phasor, discarding the redundant negative-frequency copy, giving a genuinely complex-valued signal whose magnitude tracks the original signal's envelope and whose phase tracks its instantaneous frequency directly. Constructing it from a real signal uses the **Hilbert transform** (a 90° phase shift applied to every frequency component) to build the correct imaginary part. You won't need the Hilbert transform's mechanics for the rest of this course, but the core idea — a real signal's spectrum is inherently mirror-symmetric, so half of it is redundant — is exactly the kind of structural symmetry you'll be watching for throughout.
screens.selfExplainPrompt: In your own words: why does a real-valued signal always have a mirror-symmetric spectrum (paired positive and negative frequency components)?
screens.selfExplainAnswer: A strong answer notes that any real signal can be written as a sum of terms like cos(ωt) = ½(e^{jωt} + e^{-jωt}), which always contributes equally to both +ω and −ω. There's no way to build a genuinely real-valued oscillation from complex exponentials without pairing up a positive-frequency term with its conjugate at the corresponding negative frequency — so the mirror symmetry is a direct, unavoidable consequence of requiring the final signal to be real.

screens.worked: Write $\cos(2\pi \times 5t)$ as a sum of two complex exponentials: using $\cos(\omega t) = \tfrac12(e^{j\omega t}+e^{-j\omega t})$ with $\omega = 2\pi\times5 = 10\pi$, this is $\tfrac12 e^{j10\pi t} + \tfrac12 e^{-j10\pi t}$ — two phasors, each with amplitude $\tfrac12$, spinning at $+5$ Hz and $-5$ Hz respectively.
screens.prompt: Write $\cos(2\pi\times 8t)$ as $\tfrac12 e^{j\omega t} + \tfrac12 e^{-j\omega t}$ for the correct value of $\omega$. Enter $\omega$ (in rad/s, i.e. $2\pi\times 8$).
screens.successFeedback: Right — ω = 2π×8 ≈ 50.27 rad/s. Same pattern as the worked example: an 8 Hz cosine splits into two ±8 Hz phasors, each with amplitude ½.
hints: ω = 2πf where f is the frequency in Hz.
